title: Type Aliases
description: Create custom names for any type.

slides.title: The Naming Problem 🏷️
slides.content:
Imagine you have a literal union type `"pending" | "shipped" | "delivered"`.

Typing that out exactly every single time you declare a variable or a function parameter becomes repetitive and error-prone.

We need a way to wrap that union in a single reusable name.

slides.title: The 'type' Keyword
slides.content: A **Type Alias** lets you assign any valid TypeScript type—no matter how complex—to a custom name.

quizData.question: Can a Type Alias be used to name standard object shapes exactly like an Interface?

options.text: Yes, e.g. `type User = { name: string; age: number; };`
No, Type Aliases are strictly for Union types.
Yes, but they compile down to heavy JS classes.

quizData.explanation: Yes! Type Aliases and Interfaces frequently overlap in functionality. Both can define objects cleanly.

slides.title: Type vs Interface 🛡️
slides.content:
Since both `type` and `interface` can describe objects, which do you use?

The community rule of thumb: Use `interface` by default for object shapes because they have slightly better error messages and can be 'merged' later.
Use `type` for Unions, Intersections, and Primitives.

slides.title: Clean Code 🧹
slides.content: Type Aliases instantly clean up your codebase by hiding complex logical types behind incredibly readable names like `type UserId = number | string;`!
